title: How to Stop Taking On Other People's Emotions
meta_description: Stop absorbing everyone else's feelings. Learn to tell the difference between empathy and emotional takeover. Protect your energy without shutting down. Practical steps to reclaim your peace.
og_title: How to Stop Taking On Other People's Emotions
og_description: Stop absorbing everyone else's feelings. Learn to tell the difference between empathy and emotional takeover. Protect your energy without shutting down. Practical steps to reclaim your peace.
body:
You're Not Broken. You're Just Noticed.

Let me guess. You walk into a room and within seconds you feel it. The tension between two people who aren't speaking. The quiet sadness your coworker is trying to hide. The anxiety that's buzzing under the surface of a group chat. And before you know it, you're carrying all of it. You're exhausted, irritable, and you can't figure out why. Sound familiar?

Here's the thing nobody told you: you're not broken. You're not "too sensitive." You're not making it up. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most people's. You pick up on emotional frequencies that others don't even register. That's not a flaw. It's a feature. But right now, it's running your life instead of serving you.

I'm Kalesh. I've been where you are. I used to think I was defective because I could feel other people's emotions like they were my own. I'd leave social events feeling drained, confused, and sometimes angry for no reason I could name. It took me years to understand that I wasn't absorbing their emotions. I was just too open to them. And the good news? You can learn to close the door without building a wall.

Let's talk about how to stop taking on other people's emotions - without becoming cold, without losing your empathy, and without pretending you don't feel what you feel.

"The boundary between your emotions and someone else's is not a wall. It's a gate. You get to decide who comes in, when, and for how long."

Why You're Taking On Emotions in the First Place

Before we get into the how, we need to talk about the why. Because if you don't understand why you're doing this, you'll keep repeating the pattern no matter how many techniques you try.

You're taking on other people's emotions because somewhere along the way, you learned that your safety depended on it. Think about it. When you were a kid, did you have to manage a parent's mood to feel safe? Did you learn to read the room before you could read a book? Did you get praised for being "the easy one" or "the helper"?

That's the bright wound. Your sensitivity - the very thing that makes you intuitive, compassionate, and deeply aware - was shaped by survival. You learned to pick up on emotions because it kept you safe. But now you're an adult. You don't need that survival mechanism running 24/7 anymore. It's like having an alarm system that still goes off when a leaf blows past the window. It worked once. Now it's just noise.

The first step is recognizing that you're not taking on emotions because you're weak. You're doing it because you're highly attuned. And that attunement can be redirected. Right?!

The Difference Between Empathy and Sympathy

Here's a distinction that changed everything for me. There's a difference between feeling with someone and feeling for someone. Empathy is feeling with - you sense what they're experiencing, you understand it, you're present with it. Sympathy is feeling for - you take on their emotion as your own, you carry it, you try to fix it.

Most sensitive people have been trained to collapse these two things. You think if you're not feeling their pain, you're not being compassionate. But that's a lie. You can be deeply present with someone's grief, anger, or fear without making it yours. In fact, you're more helpful when you don't take it on. Because when you're drowning in their emotion, you can't offer anything clear. You're just two drowning people holding each other down.

Try this the next time someone is upset. Instead of thinking "I feel their pain," shift to "I see their pain." That small language change - from feel to see - creates a tiny bit of distance. And distance is not coldness. Distance is clarity.

Your Emotional Immune System

Think of your sensitivity like an immune system. A healthy immune system doesn't let everything in. It's selective. It recognizes what's yours and what's not. It knows when to respond and when to rest. But right now, your emotional immune system is wide open. Everything gets in. And just like a physical immune system that's constantly fighting off invaders, you're exhausted.

Building an emotional immune system means learning to filter. And the first filter is simple: Is this mine?

When you feel a sudden wave of emotion - especially if it seems to come out of nowhere or doesn't match what you were just feeling - stop and ask: "Is this mine?" If the answer is no, you don't have to do anything with it. You can acknowledge it, name it, and let it pass through you like a cloud. You don't have to grab onto it. You don't have to fix it. You don't have to make it go away. You just have to let it be there without making it yours.

I recommend keeping a small journal for a week. Every time you notice an emotion that feels like it might not be yours, write it down. What were you feeling? Where were you? Who were you with? Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that certain people drain you more. That certain environments hit you harder. That's your map. Use it.

Grounding: The Non-Negotiable Practice

You cannot stop taking on other people's emotions if you're not grounded. It's like trying to stand steady in a hurricane while holding a sail. It's not going to work. Grounding is the anchor that keeps you in your own body, your own energy, your own life.

Here's a grounding practice you can do right now. Sit with your feet flat on the floor. Close your eyes if that's comfortable. Take three slow breaths. Then imagine roots growing from the soles of your feet down into the earth. Feel them going deep - past the floor, past the foundation, down into the ground. Let them anchor you. Now, imagine any energy that isn't yours draining down through those roots and into the earth. The earth can handle it. It's been processing energy for billions of years.

Do this for two minutes before you walk into a social situation. Do it after a difficult conversation. Do it when you wake up and before you go to sleep. It's not fancy. It's not complicated. But it works. And it's the foundation everything else is built on.

If you want a deeper practice, I recommend the book The Empath's Survival Guide by Judith Orloff. It's full of practical tools for people who feel everything. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Shield Technique (Not What You Think)

A lot of people talk about "shielding" - imagining a protective bubble around you. I've never been a fan of that approach. Here's why: shields block everything. They block the difficult emotions, sure. But they also block the love, the joy, the connection. They isolate you. And that's not what you want.

Instead, I teach a different kind of shield. Imagine a soft, permeable membrane around your body. It's like a filter. It lets in what's useful and holds back what's not. You can think of it like a window screen. It keeps the bugs out but lets the breeze in. Or like a sieve. It catches the rocks but lets the water through.

The key is that you get to decide what the filter allows. You get to set the mesh size. And you can adjust it depending on the situation. With a close friend who's grieving, you might open it a little wider so you can be present. In a crowded room full of strangers, you might tighten it up so you don't get overwhelmed. You're not shutting down. You're discriminating.

Practice this by setting your filter intentionally before you go into any situation. Say to yourself: "I allow in only what serves my highest good and the highest good of all involved. Everything else passes through me without attachment." Does that land?

Name It to Tame It

One of the fastest ways to stop taking on emotions is to name them. When you feel a sudden spike of anxiety that you know isn't yours, say to yourself (out loud if you're alone, silently if you're not): "That's not mine. That's someone else's anxiety." Just naming it creates separation. It tells your brain: "This is not my problem."

You can get even more specific. "That's my mother's worry about money." "That's my partner's frustration with their boss." "That's the collective tension in this meeting." The more precise you are, the easier it is to let go.

I also recommend using your hands. When you feel something that isn't yours, physically brush it off your body. Sweep your hands down your arms, your chest, your legs. Imagine you're brushing off dust. It sounds silly. It works. Your body responds to physical cues. Give it a physical signal that you're releasing what isn't yours.

The People Who Drain You

Let's be honest. Some people are emotional vampires. They don't mean to be. They're not bad people. But they offload their emotions onto anyone who will hold them. And if you're a sensitive person, you're a prime target. You feel their pain, you want to help, and before you know it, you're carrying their entire emotional load.

You don't have to cut these people out of your life. But you do need to set limits. Here's how:

  • Limit your time. You don't have to stay in a draining conversation for hours. Ten minutes is enough. Set a timer if you have to.
  • Limit your availability. You don't have to answer every call, every text, every plea. You are not on call 24/7. Turn off notifications. Let things go to voicemail.
  • Limit your emotional investment. You can care about someone without taking on their feelings. Say: "I hear you. That sounds really hard. I'm here with you." That's enough. You don't need to fix it, solve it, or feel it.

For more on this, check out Emotional Freedom by Judith Orloff. It's a complete guide to navigating relationships when you're highly sensitive. See on Amazon (paid link)

Your Body Is Your Best Guide

Your body knows when you're taking on someone else's emotions before your mind does. Learn to read the signals. When you're around someone, check in with your body. Is your chest tight? Is your stomach knotted? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Do you feel heavy, sluggish, or suddenly exhausted? Those are clues.

Your body is giving you real-time feedback. It's saying: "Hey, something here isn't mine. Something here is draining me." Listen to it. When you notice those signals, take a step back. Physically move away if you can. Take a breath. Shake out your hands. Do whatever it takes to reset.

One of the most powerful tools I've found is the Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not specifically about empathy, but it's essential reading for anyone who wants to understand how the body holds emotions. See on Amazon (paid link)

What to Do When You've Already Taken It On

Sometimes you don't catch it in time. You're already carrying someone else's anger, sadness, or anxiety. It's sitting in your chest like a rock. What do you do then?

First, don't judge yourself. You're not a failure. You're learning. The fact that you noticed means you're already more aware than you were before.

Second, release it. You can do this through movement. Shake your body. Jump up and down. Go for a run. Dance. Move the energy out. You can also do it through breath. Take a deep breath in, and as you exhale, imagine blowing the emotion out of your body. Do it several times until you feel lighter.

Third, wash it off. Take a shower. Imagine the water carrying away everything that isn't yours. Water is incredibly cleansing for empaths. Even just splashing cold water on your face and wrists can help.

Fourth, give it back. This might sound strange, but you can mentally return the emotion to its owner. Say: "This belongs to you. I'm giving it back with love." You don't need to do this out loud or in their presence. You can do it in your mind. The intention is enough.

The Gift of Discernment

Here's the truth that changed my life: you don't have to feel everything. You get to choose. Discernment is the ability to tell the difference between what's yours and what's not, and to act on that knowledge. It's the highest expression of empathy. Because when you're not drowning in other people's emotions, you can actually show up for them in a way that matters.

Think of it like this. You're standing on a shore. The ocean is full of emotions - yours and everyone else's. You used to jump in and let the waves toss you around. Now you're learning to stand on the shore, to observe the waves, to choose when to wade in and when to stay dry. You're not disconnected. You're discerning.

That's the goal. Not to stop feeling. But to feel on purpose. To feel what's yours, to be present with what's not, and to let it go. That's the bright wound healing. That's you stepping into your power.

For a deeper dive into this entire process, I recommend Thriving as an Empath by Judith Orloff. It's a companion guide to the survival guide, with more advanced practices. See on Amazon (paid link)

Your Next Step

You've been carrying other people's emotions for a long time. It's become a habit. But habits can be broken. And new ones can be built. Start small. Pick one practice from this article and try it today. Maybe it's the grounding exercise. Maybe it's asking "Is this mine?" Maybe it's naming the emotion and brushing it off. Whatever it is, do it. Then do it again tomorrow. And the day after that.

You're not going to get this perfect. You're going to slip. You're going to take on emotions you didn't mean to. That's okay. Every time you catch yourself, you're strengthening the muscle. Every time you choose to release what isn't yours, you're reclaiming your energy.

And remember: you're not alone in this. There are millions of people just like you, learning to work through this world with their sensitivity intact. You're part of a tribe. The Bright Wound tribe. And we're here for you.
